page: Alibaba Cloud ecs.u2i-c1m4.2xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance powered by the KVM hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture with an Intel Xeon processor running at a clock speed of 2.7 GHz. The instance is configured with 8 dedicated vCPUs mapped to 8 physical cores, meaning there is 1.0 thread per core with no hyperthreading. It includes 32.0 GB of system memory, providing a balanced allocation of 4.0 GB of RAM per core.

This instance does not include any local storage or bundled GPUs, requiring users to attach external block storage for operating system and application data. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 5 Gbps. There are no complimentary public IPv4 addresses included with this instance type.

Given its dedicated CPU allocation and standard 1:4 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, the ecs.u2i-c1m4.2xlarge offers a stable resource profile for general-purpose workloads. The absence of hyperthreading ensures predictable compute performance per core, making it suitable for applications sensitive to CPU scheduling latency. Recommended workloads include mid-tier web servers, application servers, containerized microservices, and small to medium database instances that do not require high-performance local NVMe storage or GPU acceleration.
description: Alibaba Cloud ecs.u2i-c1m4.2xlarge is a general-purpose virtual machine instance built on the KVM hypervisor. It features an Intel Xeon x86_64 processor operating at 2.7 GHz, configured with 8 dedicated vCPUs across 8 physical cores with no hyperthreading. The instance is equipped with 32.0 GB of RAM, establishing a balanced 4.0 GB per core ratio. It does not include local storage or integrated GPUs, meaning all storage requirements must be met via network-attached block storage. Network performance is defined by a baseline bandwidth of 5 Gbps, and no complimentary public IPv4 addresses are provided. This hardware configuration is designed for predictable, dedicated compute performance, making it well-suited for general-purpose workloads such as application servers, containerized microservices, and mid-sized databases.
